deductions.reason: Across all three conversations, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly—i.e., no rationale is given for the Socratic/guided-discovery approach in terms of learning benefits, retention, or skill development. The tutor enforces constraints consistently but never articulates why this supports the student's learning.

In all three transcripts, when students improve their reasoning (e.g., moving from vague impressions to defensible ratings), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked better. No turn explicitly asks the student to reflect on their own process improvement.

grade.overview: The tutor performs very well across all three transcripts. Socratic method is consistently applied—students are asked to generate their own stressors, classifications, ratings, and justifications before the tutor responds. No submission-ready content is provided; the tutor explicitly tells students to write their own drafts. Scaffolding is strong: the tutor builds on student progress turn by turn, uses diagnostic questions, and advances the conversation methodically. The main weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the guided-discovery approach (1.3.A) and the lack of process-reflection prompts after student improvement (1.3.C). Communication is concise and appropriately supportive throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions warranted. The tutor never provides final answers, never rewrites student text, and consistently asks students to generate ideas before confirming or redirecting. Key reasoning steps remain student-led across all three conversations. 1.2: No deductions. The tutor builds on each student's prior responses, acknowledges correct steps, and uses diagnostic questions before addressing confusion. 1.3: Two deductions applied. First, the tutor never explains why it is using a question-based approach rather than giving direct answers—no learning rationale is offered in any of the three conversations (1.3.A.a, -2 pts). Second, when students improve (e.g., moving from vague to evidence-based justifications), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in their thinking (1.3.C.a, -2 pts). Method-level feedback is present and specific, so 1.3.B is not penalized. Dialogue quality and communication quality are strong with no evidence of repetition, drift, off-topic engagement, monologuing, or tone issues.
